details.text: Derivative: إِنَّا which is composed of إِنَّ (Inna) which is used for the purpose of تَوْكِيد (Emphasis) and to remove doubt from a matter or issue and نَا (naa) which is a pronoun with the meaning here of “We”. إِنَّا (Innaa) therefore means: “No doubt, We” and both parts act to reinforce and emphasise the statement that follows it. The use of this expression can also imply specificity and specialness with the meaning of: “No doubt, it is only We” just as it implies providing great importance to the statement that follows.

Derivative: الْكَوْثَرَ comes from the فَوْعَان (Faw’aan) pattern which is in a صِيغَة المُبَالَغَة (Seeghat ul-Mubaalaghah) form which is the accentuated and hyperbolised form. It implies increased power and emphasis which in contrast to exaggeration which means to exceed beyond the truth or reality and to stretch it. The Qur’an includes a lot of hyperbole but does not include any exaggeration. الْكَوْثَرَ (Al-Kauthar) implies incredible amounts of something. كَثِير (Katheer) means a lot whilst كَوْثَر means an incredible amount or quantity i.e., even more than a lot. In general terms it refers here to a great abundance of good. كَوْثَر (Kauthar) was used to by the Arabs to describe large quantities of something or something of great value. Unlike كَثِير (Katheer - a lot) which can be used to describe good and bad things, كَوْثَر is only used for good things that are in abundance. The الْ (Alif Laam) attached to كَوْثَر in الْكَوْثَرَ can indicate to specifying something in terms of one particular thing. In that case الْكَوْثَرَ (Al-Kauthar) can refer alone to the الحَوْض (Al-Hawd – River in Jannah). The usage of الْ (Alif Laam) upon a noun which is being used as an adjective it can also imply absoluteness and being unrestricted in the Arabic language. This can mean that الْكَوْثَرَ (Al-Kauthar) is not limited or restricted to the الحَوْض (Al-Hawd – River in Jannah) but rather encompasses the favours which Allah provided the Messenger of Allah ﷺ with. The two meanings of Al-Kauthar can be reconciled by stating that الحَوْض (Al-Hawd – River in Jannah) is from Al-Kauthar (the great abundance of good) which Allah gave to the Messenger ﷺ in terms of gifts and favours.

Derivative: أَعْطَيْنَا means we gave from the word أَعْطَى – إِعْطاء. It has been used in the Qur’an to refer to something that is given attached to responsibilities or to something that can be taken back after it has been given. إِعْطاء (to give or giving) can refer to when someone is given something as a result of their obedience. It is not the same as a reward or recompense but rather when giving is undertaken without expectation for a reward. إِعْطاء (to give or giving) is also usually used for big things and not insignificant things. It can also refer to a favour that cannot be compared with and something that is given freely without strings attached to enjoy or benefit from. أَعْطَيْنَا “We have given” is in the past tense (المَاضِي – Al-Maadiy) however its meaning has to be understood to be talking about the future. The past tense in Arabic refers to imply that something is for sure and guaranteed. If it is intended to talk about something that is in the future which is certain or guaranteed, then the past tense is used. The past tense also implies completion in the case where the present tense is used in respect to an act that has yet to be completed. The giving here therefore implies that it will be complete, in full and not shortchanged in any way.
Derivative: صَلِّ “Pray” – It is said that the prayer is the most comprehensive way to show gratitude to Allah and to thank Him for His favours.
Derivative: فَ – The فَاء (Faa’ – فَ) is to connect in order what was stated after it with what came stated before it.

Derivative: انْحَرْ is from the word نَحَرَ – نَحْرٌ which literally means to cut in the throat and generally refers to slaughtering an animal in sacrifice. The majority view is that this relates to the slaughtering and sacrifice undertaken on the day of Eid ul-Ad’haa.

Derivative: شَانِئ comes from the word شَنَأَ – شَنَآن which means to hate. شَانِئ refers to the one who hates someone or something. شَنَأَ can include hatred and animosity, in addition to implying a hatred that is partially hidden.
Derivative: إِنَّ is used to remove doubt i.e., “There is no doubt that such and such”.

Derivative: الْأَبْتَرُ comes from the word بَتَرَ – بَتْرٌ which was used to refer to cutting the tail of an animal off and severing it from its body. It carries the connotation of humiliation. A great source of humiliation for an Arab was for his future generations not to carry his name and as such they used this word with its humiliating connotation in this context i.e., in respect to his line being cut off as a result of having no sons. الْأَبْتَرُ (Al-Abtar) is the one who is most cut off or even more cut off. Being the one who is truly cut off implies being cut off from all true good and benefit.

إِنَّ شَانِئَكَ هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ

“Indeed (no doubt) (إِنَّ), the one who hates you (شَانِئَكَ), (it is) he (هُوَ), who is the most cut off (الْأَبْتَرُ)”. The use of هُوَ in هُوَ الْأَبْتَرُ “(It is) he who is the most cut off” implies: “And not you” i.e., It is “he” and not you who is truly cut off.
